1: When Rachel saw that she was not bearing Jacob any children, she became jealous of her sister and said to Jacob, “Give me children, or I’ll die!” — Rachel’s jealousy of Leah’s fruitfulness and her desperate demand for children. The desperate longing for children that feels like a longing for life itself connects to the deeper spiritual longing for new birth: “unless a man is born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God” (John 3:3). The barren woman who cries for life is the type of the spiritually barren soul who cries to Christ for the life that only the Spirit gives. “Whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for me will find it” (Matthew 16:25) — the life that Rachel grasps at is the life that only God can give.
2: Jacob became angry with her and said, “Am I in the place of God, who has kept you from having children?” — Jacob’s theologically correct response: only God opens the womb. The recognition that fertility is in God’s hands, not the husband’s, is the OT’s consistent theological point (Genesis 16:2; Genesis 25:21; Genesis 29:31). Christ applies the same theological logic to spiritual birth: “The wind blows wherever it pleases. You hear its sound, but you cannot tell where it comes from or where it is going. So it is with everyone born of the Spirit” (John 3:8). The Spirit births new life by divine initiative, not human effort. Jacob is not in the place of God; Christ is not “in the place of God” but is God (John 1:1; Genesis 8:58), and it is precisely this that makes his giving of life authoritative.
3: Rachel said, “Here is Bilhah, my servant. Sleep with her so that she can bear children for me” — Rachel’s Bilhah-solution is the Sarai/Hagar pattern repeated: human engineering to produce what God has promised to give in his own time and way. Paul’s allegory in Galatians 4:21–31 identifies this “according to the flesh” route as the type of law-righteousness: human effort to achieve what only divine promise can supply. The human alternative to the divine promise consistently produces not the covenant inheritance but a counterfeit that complicates rather than resolves. Only God’s opening of Rachel’s womb (v22) gives the true heir; only faith in Christ gives the true righteousness.
4: She gave him her servant Bilhah as a wife. Jacob slept with her — Jacob’s compliance with the Bilhah arrangement. The covenant-bearer’s moral complexity — complying with an arrangement that multiplies wives and complicates the household — is a reminder that the covenant promise does not depend on its bearers’ moral perfection. God’s covenant purposes work through and despite human moral failure. The NT is explicit about this: Christ’s genealogy (Matthew 1:1–17) includes Tamar (who played a harlot), Rahab (a Canaanite prostitute), Ruth (a Moabite), and Bathsheba (the wife of Uriah). The covenant runs through moral failure to the one who has no moral failure of his own.
5: Bilhah became pregnant and bore Jacob a son — the birth through the surrogate. The surrogate-birth is the “according to the flesh” route to family; the Spirit-birth is the covenant route. The surrogate son is real and loved — Dan is one of the twelve tribes — but is not the child of primary promise. The distinction between genuine gifts that fall short of the central promise and the central promise itself runs through Genesis. Christ is not one among several religious options (surrogates) but the one through whom alone the promise is fulfilled: “Salvation is found in no one else” (Acts 4:12).
6: Rachel said, “God has vindicated me; he has listened to my plea and given me a son.” Because of this she named him Dan — Dan (“he has vindicated / judged”). The divine vindication of the one who was failing — God heard Rachel’s plea — is a type of the divine vindication that Christ receives at the resurrection. 1 Timothy 3:16: Christ was “vindicated by the Spirit” — the resurrection is the Father’s vindication of the Son who was apparently defeated. The God who vindicates Rachel by giving her a son vindicates Christ by raising him from the dead: “God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him” (Acts 2:24).
7: Rachel’s servant Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son — the second son through the surrogate. The twin sons through Bilhah (Dan and Naphtali) correspond to the twin sons through Zilpah (Gad and Asher, vv10–13). The four surrogate sons alongside the six sons of Leah and the two sons of Rachel form the twelve tribes. The twelve who come through such complex and morally fraught family arrangements become the twelve who are the foundation of God’s covenant people — and ultimately the twelve apostles who are the foundation of the new covenant community (Ephesians 2:20; Revelation 21:14).
8: Rachel said, “I have had a great struggle with my sister, and I have won.” So she named him Naphtali — Naphtali (“my struggle / wrestling”). The competitive struggle between Leah and Rachel — the beloved and the unloved, fighting for children and therefore for honor — is the fallen-family context of the covenant. The struggling that Rachel names her son after is the type of the spiritual warfare that runs through the covenant community’s history. Christ’s victory in Gethsemane (“Not my will, but yours be done,” Luke 22:42) is the supreme wrestling-and-winning that Naphtali’s name anticipates: the victor who wins by submission.
9: When Leah saw that she had stopped having children, she took her servant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife — Leah’s Zilpah-arrangement mirrors Rachel’s Bilhah-arrangement: both wives using human engineering when the divine gift seems to have paused. The competitive human engineering of the two wives is the type of the “works” approach that Paul contrasts with faith: “not by works, but by him who calls” (Romans 9:11). The covenant community’s temptation is consistently to engineer by human effort what God has promised to provide by grace through faith in Christ.
10: Leah’s servant Zilpah bore Jacob a son — the third surrogate son (second through Zilpah). The sheer complexity of the covenant family’s child-bearing arrangements — four women, twelve sons, one daughter — is the morally tangled context in which God’s covenant purposes move forward. The God who works in and through such complexity is the God of the incarnation, who enters into the full messiness of human family life: “born of a woman, born under the law” (Galatians 4:4), not above the tangle but within it.
11: Then Leah said, “What good fortune!” So she named him Gad — Gad (“good fortune / troop”). The acknowledgment of divine blessing in the birth — “good fortune” — is the appropriate response to God’s provision. The NT equivalent is “every good and perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of the heavenly lights” (James 1:17). The recognition that blessing comes from God rather than from human effort is the theological posture that Christ calls his disciples to: “Apart from me you can do nothing” (John 15:5).
12: Leah’s servant Zilpah bore Jacob a second son — the fourth surrogate son. Each of these births, through their complexity, adds to the tribal inventory that becomes Israel’s twelve-tribe structure. The messy multiplicity that the narrative records is the raw material of God’s covenant ordering. Paul sees the whole of Israel’s complex history as the preparatory ground for Christ: “I could wish that I myself were cursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my people… Theirs is the adoption to sonship; theirs the divine glory, the covenants, the receiving of the law… theirs the patriarchs, and from them is traced the human ancestry of the Messiah” (Romans 9:3–5).
13: Then Leah said, “How happy I am! The women will call me happy.” So she named him Asher — Asher (“happy / blessed”). The declaration of blessedness by the unloved wife — “How happy I am!” — is the OT anticipation of the beatitudes: “Blessed are the poor in spirit… blessed are those who mourn‫ blessed are the meek” (Matthew 5:3–5). The happiness that Leah declares despite her unloved status is the type of the joy that Christ brings to those the world overlooks: “Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God” (Luke 6:20). The unloved Leah, made happy by God’s gift, is the type of the “poor in spirit” who inherit the kingdom.
14: Reuben went out into the fields during wheat harvest and found some mandrake plants, which he brought to his mother Leah. Rachel said to Leah, “Please give me some of your son’s mandrakes” — the mandrakes (thought to have fertility properties in the ANE) are the human fertility-seeking that contrasts with divine gift. Rachel seeks mandrakes to secure what only God can give; she receives Joseph not through the mandrakes but through God’s remembering (v22). The futility of the fertility-magic is the type of all human effort to achieve by technique what only divine grace provides: “Not by might nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the LORD Almighty” (Zechariah 4:6). Christ gives what no mandrake can: the Spirit who produces life in the spiritually barren.
15: Leah said, “Wasn’t it enough that you took away my husband? Will you take my son’s mandrakes too?” Rachel replied, “Very well; he can sleep with you tonight in return for your son’s mandrakes” — the mandrake-negotiation reduces Jacob to a commodity traded between his competing wives. The covenant-bearer bartered for herbs by his competing wives is a low point in the narrative. The moral dysfunction of the household is the context in which God’s purposes must proceed — not the ideal context but the actual one. Christ enters the actual: “he was numbered with the transgressors” (Isaiah 53:12; Mark 15:28). God’s covenant does not wait for ideal human conditions but works within the actual fallen ones.
16: Leah met Jacob coming in from the fields and said, “You must sleep with me. I have hired you with my son’s mandrakes” — Jacob hired by his own wife with mandrakes; the reduction of covenant love to transaction. The commercial reduction of covenant relationship — the opposite of the freely-given love of God — is the world’s consistent distortion of grace. Paul contrasts this with the gospel: “to the one who works, wages are not credited as a gift but as an obligation. However, to the one who does not work but trusts God who justifies the ungodly, their faith is credited as righteousness” (Romans 4:4–5). The transactional approach that reduces relationship to wages is exactly what grace overturns in Christ.
17: God listened to Leah, and she became pregnant and bore Jacob a fifth son — God listens to Leah and opens her womb — not in response to the mandrakes but in response to Leah herself. The divine hearing that goes around the human technique (the mandrakes that Rachel acquired) to respond directly to the person in need is the pattern of grace: it responds to the person, not the technique. The mandrakes avail Rachel nothing; God opens Leah’s womb because God listened to Leah. Christ’s response to prayer similarly bypasses technique: “This kind can come out only by prayer” (Mark 9:29). The direct divine response to the person’s cry, not to any ritual technique, is the shape of gospel prayer.
18: Leah said, “God has rewarded me for giving my servant to my husband.” So she named him Issachar — Issachar (“reward / wages”). The divine reward for faithfulness — even the mixed faithfulness of giving a servant-wife — is noted with gratitude. The concept of divine reward is carefully maintained in the NT as an aspect of grace, not of merit: “Each person will be rewarded according to their own labor” (1 Corinthians 3:8), but the reward comes through and not apart from Christ’s atoning work. The blessing that Leah calls “reward” is God’s free gift to the one who sought him, anticipating the promise of reward to those who seek Christ: “he rewards those who earnestly seek him” (Hebrews 11:6).
19: Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son — the sixth son of Leah. The counting of sons — first through sixth for Leah, with the surrogate sons interspersed — is the genealogical precision that ensures no tribe is missed. The careful counting of the twelve builds the structure that Jesus reconstitutes in the twelve apostles (Matthew 10:2–4). The “twelve” of Israel and the “twelve” of the apostles are explicitly linked in Matthew 19:28 and Revelation 21:12–14: the twelve gates bear the names of the twelve tribes; the twelve foundations bear the names of the twelve apostles. Both twelves point to Christ who stands at the center of both Israel and the church.
20: Leah said, “God has presented me with a precious gift. This time my husband will treat me with honor, because I have borne him six sons.” She named him Zebulun — Zebulun (“honor / dwelling”). Leah’s hope that six sons will finally bring her husband’s honor is the poignant longing of the unloved for recognition. The longing to be honored by one’s spouse is fulfilled in Christ’s love for the church: “Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved the church” (Ephesians 5:25). Christ honors his people: “To him who overcomes, I will give… I will acknowledge their name before my Father” (Revelation 3:5). The unloved Leah longing for honor receives it from the God who sees her; Christ gives the church the honor that the world withholds.
21: Some time later she gave birth to a daughter and named her Dinah — Dinah (“vindication / justice”) is born to Leah. The daughter born to the unloved wife will be the subject of the tragedy in Genesis 34 (Dinah’s assault and its aftermath). The vulnerability of the covenant family’s daughter in the surrounding culture is a type of the vulnerability of all who are “foreigners and strangers” (1 Peter 2:11) in the world. Christ is the defender of the vulnerable: “he has looked on the humble estate of his servant” (Luke 1:48); “he was moved with compassion” for those who had no shepherd (Matthew 9:36).
22: Then God remembered Rachel; he listened to her and enabled her to conceive — “God remembered Rachel” — the same divine-remembering verb (zakar) as Genesis 8:1 (“God remembered Noah”) and Exodus 2:24 (“God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob”). The divine remembering is not the end of forgetting but the beginning of covenant action: God remembered Noah → sent the wind; God remembered Rachel → opened her womb. The God who remembers is the God who acts. The fullest expression of divine remembering is the incarnation: “he has helped his servant Israel, remembering to be merciful to Abraham and his descendants forever” (Luke 1:54–55). God’s remembering of Rachel culminates in the birth of Joseph, the richest Christ-type in Genesis.
23: She became pregnant and gave birth to a son and said, “God has taken away my disgrace” — the removal of the barren woman’s disgrace by the divine gift of a son. Elizabeth uses nearly identical language at John the Baptist’s birth: “The Lord has done this for me… In these days he has shown his favor and taken away my disgrace among the people” (Luke 1:25). The removal of disgrace through divine gift — the barren woman made fruitful, the sinner made righteous — is the gospel’s consistent pattern. Christ removes the disgrace of sin: “since we have a great high priest… let us then approach God’s throne of grace with confidence” (Hebrews 4:14–16). The disgrace that barrenness (physical or spiritual) brings is taken away by the divine gift of life.
24: She named him Joseph, and said, “May the LORD add to me another son” — Joseph (“may he add / God will add”). The birth of Joseph begins the narrative that occupies the rest of Genesis (Genesis 37–50) and constitutes the OT’s richest sustained type of Christ: the beloved son sent to his brothers, rejected and sold into slavery for silver, falsely accused, imprisoned, elevated from prison to rule over Egypt, saving his brothers who had betrayed him, forgiving and providing for them from his position of power. Every major element corresponds to Christ: “God intended it for good to accomplish what is now being done, the saving of many lives” (Genesis 50:20) = “God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ” (2 Corinthians 5:19). Joseph’s name — “may God add” — is Rachel’s prayer for more; God answers with the “more” of the gospel.
25: After Rachel gave birth to Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, “Send me on my way so I can go back to my own homeland” — the birth of Joseph triggers Jacob’s desire to return to Canaan — the covenant land. The completion of the family through Joseph’s birth prompts the return journey. The pattern of departure, exile, completion, and return runs through the OT’s exile-and-restoration theology and reaches its fullest expression in the incarnation-and-ascension: Christ descends from the Father, completes his mission, and returns: “I came from the Father and entered the world; now I am leaving the world and going back to the Father” (John 16:28). The covenant-bearer’s return to his homeland is the type of Christ’s return to the Father and his people’s ultimate homecoming (John 14:2–3).
26: Jacob said, “Give me my wives and children, for whom I have served you, and I will be on my way. You know how much work I’ve done for you” — Jacob’s claim on his family — those for whom he has served — is the bridegroom claiming his own. The NT develops this language for Christ’s claim on the church: “Do you not know that your bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit… You are not your own; you were bought at a price” (1 Corinthians 6:19–20). Christ claims the church because he has served for her: “having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end” (John 13:1). The service-and-claim logic of Jacob’s 14-year labor is fulfilled in Christ’s atoning work and his claim of the church as his own.
27: Laban said, “If I have found favor in your eyes, please stay. I have learned by divination that the LORD has blessed me because of you” — even Laban recognizes by his own methods (divination) that God has blessed him through Jacob. The blessing that flows from the covenant-bearer to the surrounding world is recognized even by those who misuse and exploit the covenant-bearer. The NT makes this explicit: the presence of the covenant community in the world is a blessing even for those who are hostile: “they saw that the LORD was with him” (Genesis 39:3, of Joseph in Potiphar’s house). Christ’s presence through his people blesses the world even when the world does not acknowledge the source.
28: He added, “Name your price, and I will pay it” — Laban offers to pay whatever Jacob asks to keep him. The world’s recognition of the covenant-bearer’s value is not the same as covenant commitment. Laban wants Jacob’s blessing-producing presence without covenant loyalty. The world’s desire for the benefits of God’s blessing without the commitment of faith is the recurring temptation that Christ addresses: those who want Jesus’s bread-multiplication (John 6:26: “you are looking for me, not because you saw the signs I performed but because you ate the loaves”) but not the demands of discipleship. “Name your price” is the world’s transactional approach to the covenant relationship.
29: Jacob said, “You know how I have worked for you and how your livestock has fared under my care” — Jacob’s account of his faithful stewardship. The steward who can account for his work is the type of Christ who gave account of his completed mission: “I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do” (John 17:4). The steward’s accountability — being able to point to what was accomplished under his care — is the model of faithful service that Christ commends in the parables (Matthew 25:20–21: “Master, you entrusted me with five bags of gold. See, I have gained five more”) and fulfills in his own life.
30: “The little you had before I came has increased greatly, and the LORD has blessed you wherever I have been” — Jacob’s testimony to the blessing that has come through him. The covenant-bearer as the channel of divine blessing to the surrounding world is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise: “all peoples on earth will be blessed through you” (Genesis 12:3). The NT applies this to Christ: “For no matter how many promises God has made, they are ‘Yes’ in Christ” (2 Corinthians 1:20); “Christ has become a servant of the Jews on behalf of God’s truth, so that the promises made to the patriarchs might be confirmed and… that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy” (Romans 15:8–9).
31: Jacob replied, “Don’t give me anything. But if you will do this one thing for me, I will go on tending your flocks and watching over them” — Jacob’s generous negotiating position: no payment, just access to the flocks on specific terms. The covenant-bearer’s generosity toward the one who has exploited him is the type of Christ’s grace toward sinners: “when we were God’s enemies, we were reconciled to him through the death of his Son” (Romans 5:10). The one who has been wronged offers generous terms to the wrongdoer, not demanding retribution but proposing covenant. This is the logic of the gospel: not “pay what you owe” but “receive what you don’t deserve.”
32: “Let me go through all your flocks today and remove from them every speckled or spotted sheep, every dark-colored lamb… these will be my wages” — Jacob proposes the speckled-and-spotted animals as his wage, apparently a low-probability outcome from uniform Laban flocks. The covenant-bearer trusting God’s provision through an apparently unfavorable arrangement is the type of faith: “Now faith is confidence in what we hope for and assurance about what we do not see” (Hebrews 11:1). The arrangement that looks unpromising by human calculation but is entrusted to divine provision is the shape of covenant faith — and the shape of the cross, which looks like defeat by human calculation but is the means of cosmic victory.
33: “And my honesty will testify for me in the future, whenever you check on the wages you have paid me. Any goat in my possession that is not speckled or spotted… will be considered stolen” — Jacob’s willingness to be audited for honesty. The transparency and accountability of the covenant-bearer, open to inspection, is the type of Christ’s claim: “Can any of you prove me guilty of sin?” (John 8:46). The integrity that welcomes examination — “my honesty will testify for me” — is the character of the one who has nothing to hide. Christ’s holiness is precisely this: open to every scrutiny, found without blemish (1 Peter 1:19: “a lamb without blemish or defect”).
34: Laban said, “Agreed; let it be as you have said” — the covenant agreement is confirmed on Jacob’s terms. The one who has exploited the covenant-bearer now agrees to the arrangement that will result in the covenant-bearer’s prosperity. The reversal — the exploiter becomes the one who enriches the exploited through his own agreement — is the gospel’s pattern. The powers that crucified Christ thereby provided the means of universal salvation: “None of the rulers of this age understood it, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory” (1 Corinthians 2:8). Satan’s exploitation of Christ becomes, by divine wisdom, the means of Satan’s own defeat.
35: That same day Laban removed all the streaked or spotted male goats and all the speckled or spotted female goats and all the dark-colored lambs — Laban removes the very animals that were to be Jacob’s wages, apparently ensuring Jacob gets nothing. But Genesis 31:10–12 reveals that God guided Jacob’s breeding through a dream, so Laban’s manipulation was always within God’s overruling providence. The covenant-bearer disadvantaged by human manipulation but overruled by divine providence is the type of Christ’s situation at the cross: humanly manipulated, judicially murdered — but “this man was handed over to you by God’s deliberate plan and foreknowledge” (Acts 2:23). The manipulation that appears to defeat the covenant-bearer is the means of the greater divine purpose.
36: Then Laban put a three-day journey between himself and Jacob, while Jacob continued to tend the rest of Laban’s flocks — the three-day separation between Laban and Jacob creates the space in which Jacob’s flocks will multiply. The three-day separation is yet another appearance of the three-day pattern (see Genesis 22:4; Genesis 42:18) that the NT consistently associates with the resurrection. Jacob separated from Laban for three days after which his flocks multiply; Christ separated from the disciples (dead for three days) after which the new community of the resurrection multiplies. The space of three days is consistently the space in which divine action transforms the situation.
37: Jacob took fresh-cut branches from poplar, almond and plane trees and made white stripes on them by peeling the bark — Jacob’s breeding technique of placing striped branches in the watering troughs. Genesis 31:10–12 reveals that Jacob was guided by a dream from God; the technique was divinely revealed, not merely human ingenuity. The covenant-bearer whose wisdom is divinely guided is the type of Christ “in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3). The wisdom that shapes the breeding strategy is not human cleverness but divine instruction — the same pattern as Christ who “spoke only what the Father told him” (John 12:49–50).
38: He placed the peeled branches in all the watering troughs, so that they would be directly in front of the flocks when they came to drink — the strategic placement of the branches at the point of watering. The connection of the breeding strategy to the watering troughs — the place of drink — situates the covenant-bearer’s work at the intersection of life-giving water and new generation. Christ similarly places himself at the points of spiritual life: “I am the bread of life” (John 6:35); “I am the living water” (John 4:14); “I am the resurrection” (John 11:25). The place of water is where new life is generated, in both the flocks and the people of God.
39: The flocks mated in front of the branches and bore young that were streaked or speckled or spotted — the outcome of the divinely-guided strategy: Jacob’s wages multiply as the stronger animals produce the patterned young that belong to him. The covenant-bearer’s prosperity through God’s guidance — not through his own strength but through divinely-revealed wisdom — is the pattern of spiritual fruit-bearing: “Apart from me you can do nothing… You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit — fruit that will last” (John 15:5, 16). The multiplication of the covenant-bearer’s portion is God’s work through the covenant-bearer, not the covenant-bearer’s work alone.
40: Jacob set apart the young of the flock and made them face the streaked and dark-colored animals in Laban’s flock. He kept his own flocks separate and did not put them with Laban’s animals — the separation of Jacob’s developing flock from Laban’s. The covenant community’s distinctiveness from the surrounding world is the type of the church’s calling: “Do not conform to the pattern of this world” (Romans 12:2); “Come out from them and be separate, says the Lord” (2 Corinthians 6:17); “you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God’s special possession” (1 Peter 2:9). Christ himself is “holy, blameless, pure, set apart from sinners” (Hebrews 7:26), and through his holiness calls his people into their distinctive covenant identity.
41: Whenever the stronger females were in heat, Jacob would place the branches in the troughs in front of the animals so they would mate near the branches — Jacob’s strategy of applying the branches selectively to the stronger animals. The discernment of when and where to apply the covenant strategy is the type of pastoral wisdom. Christ applies himself to the strong and the weak differently: he calls the strong to give up their strength for others (Mark 10:43–44: “whoever wants to be great among you must be your servant”) and lifts up the weak (Matthew 11:28: “Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened”). The wise application of the covenant to each person’s situation is the shape of Christ’s pastoral ministry.
42: But if the animals were weak, he would not place the branches there. So the weak animals went to Laban and the strong to Jacob — the separation of strong from weak, with the strong belonging to Jacob and the weak to Laban. The eschatological separation — sheep from goats (Matthew 25:32), wheat from tares (Matthew 13:30) — has its type in this separation. But the eschatological logic is reversed: it is not the humanly-strong who belong to Christ but the weak who trust in him. “God has chosen the weak things of the world to shame the strong” (1 Corinthians 1:27). The type’s separation by natural strength is transformed in the antitype’s separation by faith: those who recognize their weakness and trust Christ are the ones who belong to him.
43: In this way Jacob grew exceedingly prosperous and came to own large flocks, female and male servants, and camels and donkeys — Jacob’s prosperity after 20 years of Laban’s exploitation. The covenant-bearer’s ultimate prosperity despite extended exploitation is the type of Christ’s exaltation after his humiliation: “Therefore God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name” (Philippians 2:9). The seven-years-times-two of service followed by prosperity corresponds to the cross followed by the resurrection and exaltation. The covenant-bearer who suffers longest comes out most abundantly — the logic of the kingdom in which “many who are first will be last, and many who are last will be first” (Matthew 19:30).
